rationale: The cited paper’s constructs (preference revision/concession in dyadic decisions) are conceptually related to the citing paper’s multistakeholder buyer–user decision framework, so it could be a theory/mechanism source rather than a purely incidental citation. However, the available evidence is only a DOI-level reference match with no in-text citation context or stated direct use, and the empirical domains differ substantially (family dyads vs. hospital B2B buying). Given this sparse evidence, I cannot safely drop it as obvious mention-only.
